task: I’ve got a rush translation cleanup that may need to be finished with a shaky internet connection and a couple of interruptions. Can you help me set up a practical way to keep it moving and decide what to do first if I only have a short window to work on it?

turns.agent_response:
Yes — under time pressure, the safest approach is to **triage first, polish later**.

## Short-window workflow

### 1) Do a fast priority pass
Handle only the items that can change meaning:

- names
- dates
- numbers
- deadlines
- amounts
- instructions
- warnings
- repeated key terms

If one of those is unclear, mark it immediately.

### 2) Separate “must-check” from “can-wait”
Use three buckets:

| Bucket | What goes in it | Action |
|---|---|---|
| Must-check now | meaning-changing items, conflicts, illegible spots | verify or flag |
| Can wait | style, wording smoothness, minor repetition | leave for later |
| Safe to clean | obvious typos, spacing, formatting | fix now |

### 3) Work in small chunks
If you may get interrupted:
- finish one section before starting another
- note where you stopped
- write down any unresolved items before leaving the file

### 4) Keep a running note list
For anything uncertain, record:
- page / section
- issue
- what you think it is
- whether it needs source check

That way you don’t have to re-read the same spot after an interruption.

### 5) If the connection drops
Do local work only:
- cleanup you can do offline
- notes for later review
- list of questions to verify when you’re back online

## Rule of thumb
If you only have a short window, spend it on:
1. meaning risks
2. unresolved source issues
3. anything that would be hard to reconstruct later

Leave style polishing for the end.

If you want, I can turn this into a very short “10-minute / 30-minute / 1-hour” version.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that’s the right order. Triage first, polish later. That’s pretty much how I handle rush cleanup too.

The bucket idea is fine. I’d keep it even simpler:
- meaning first
- source problems next
- formatting last

If I only had a short window, I’d do this:
1. hit names, dates, numbers, amounts, deadlines
2. flag any ambiguous legal or technical wording
3. fix obvious typos and broken formatting
4. leave style alone unless it changes tone or meaning

And if I had to stop in the middle, I’d leave myself a plain note with section/page and the exact issue. Otherwise you end up rereading the same mess later. Been there.

The only thing I’d add is: don’t spend too long trying to make one ugly line perfect while the rest of the file is still uncleaned. That’s how you lose the whole window.
